blocks.text: for I give you good precepts: do not forsake my teaching.
When I was a son with my father, tender, and my mother's favorite,
he taught me, and said to me, “Let your heart hold fast my words; keep my commandments, and live.
Get wisdom; get insight: do not forget, nor turn away from the words of my mouth.
Do not forsake her, and she will keep you; love her, and she will guard you.
The beginning of wisdom is this: Get wisdom, and whatever else you get, get insight.

7 : (Heb “qanah”), i.e., to buy with cash or exchange of goods (e.g., 17.16; Gen 47.19). The verb also refers to marriage (Ruth 4.5,10). Most marriages in the ancient Near East were negotiated as business arrangements between two families.
Prize her highly, and she will exalt you; she will honor you if you embrace her.
6–8 : depicts Wisdom as a beloved wife (5.15–20; 7.4; cf. 31.10–31).
She will place on your head a fair garland; she will bestow on you a beautiful crown.”
4.1–9: Get Wisdom. The father recalls his father's instruction.
9 : , symbols of favor, authority, and marriage (cf. Isa 61.10; 62.3; Song 3.11). On Wisdom as adornment, see 1.9n.
Hear, my child, and accept my words, that the years of your life may be many.
I have taught you the way of wisdom; I have led you in the paths of uprightness.
When you walk, your step will not be hampered; and if you run, you will not stumble.
Keep hold of instruction; do not let go; guard her, for she is your life.
13 : . Reference to the masculine noun (see 1.2n.) in the feminine suggests it is a name for Wisdom (cf. 4.6–8).
Do not enter the path of the wicked, and do not walk in the way of evildoers.
Avoid it; do not go on it; turn away from it and pass on.
For they cannot sleep unless they have done wrong; they are robbed of sleep unless they have made someone stumble.
For they eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence.
17 : , what people consume, literally and metaphorically, reveals their character (e.g., 9.5,17; 23.3,6; 31.27; cf. Job 15.16; 34.7).
But the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, which shines brighter and brighter until full day.
The way of the wicked is like deep darkness; they do not know what they stumble over.
4.10–19: Two paths.
18–19 : reinforces the contrast between the two paths, as do hazards on the way of the wicked (cf. Job 18.8–11).
My child, be attentive to my words; incline your ear to my sayings.
Do not let them escape from your sight; keep them within your heart.
For they are life to those who find them, and healing to all their flesh.
Keep your heart with all vigilance, for from it flow the springs of life.
Put away from you crooked speech, and put devious talk far from you.
Let your eyes look directly forward, and your gaze be straight before you.
Keep straight the path of your feet, and all your ways will be sure.
Do not swerve to the right or to the left; turn your foot away from evil.
1.1–9.18: The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.
4.20–27: Anatomy of the wise features five organs of the body particularly important in Proverbs: ears, eyes, heart, mouth, and feet.
